coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the origin of photoluminescence in dual emissive biogenic carbon dot
coredata.dc:creator: Deb, Ankita
Chowdhury, Devasish
coredata.dc:description:
There has been considerable interest in dual emissive carbon quantum dots because of their exciting photoluminescence properties for varied applications. This work demonstrates a dual emissive system derived from a biogenic source, betalain extracted from beetroot. Different betalain carbon dots were prepared utilizing varying temperatures and time. The photoluminescence properties of
BT − CD
5
160
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 160 ℃ for 5 min) and
BT − CD
10
180
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 180 ℃ for 10 mins) were investigated in detail using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and time-resolved PL decay studies. Interestingly the emission spectra are different and possess multi-emissive nature owing to the involvement of different surface states on the CDs. Further the PL response of
BT − CD
5
160
and,
BT − CD
10
180
in the presence of triethylamine (TEA), an aliphatic amine and, diphenylamine (DPA), an aromatic amine was found to be different when studied for both λext < 400 nm and > 400 nm. While the emission obtained from
BT − CD
5
160
emerges from the aromatic carbon core and also from the functional groups attached to the carbon dots’ surface, the emission in
BT − CD
10
180
is fully governed by the surface traps and defects obtained from the functionalities present. Hence, such investigated photoluminescence studies of the origin of emission will help exploit the dual emissive behaviour of biogenic carbon dots for extensive application in ratiometric sensing and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Dual-emission
Carbon core
Surface states
PL lifetime

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author thank IASST for in-house project. The authors also thank SAIC, IASST; SAIF, CSIR-NEIST; CIF, IIT-Guwahati for instrumentation facilities. AD thanks IASST for fellowship.

BT − CD
10
180
.

BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
(b), High resolution XPS of C1s, N1s and O1s region of
BT − CD
5
160
(c, d, e) and of
BT − CD
10
180
(f, g, h).

UV–visible spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
(A), Stacked PL emission spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
(B) and,
BT − CD
10
180
(C).

Excitation spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
(a), and
BT − CD
10
180
(b).

Emission spectra response of
BT − CD
5
160
(a, b) and,
BT − CD
10
180
(c, d) at different pH medium chosen at two excitation wavelengths, < 400 nm and > 400 nm.

Response of the PL emission spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
(A, B) and,
BT − CD
10
180
(C, D) after addition of different concentrations of triethylamine (TEA) at λex < 400 nm, and > 400 nm.

Response of the PL emission spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
(a, b) and,
BT − CD
10
180
(c, d) after addition of different concentrations of diphenylamine (DPA) at λex of < 400 nm, and > 400 nm.
Various radiative pathways responsible for the PL emissions of the BT-CDs.

Lifetimes (τ) of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
at different excitation wavelengths.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The %values in parentheses () represent the fractional contribution of each of lifetime components.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the origin of photoluminescence in dual emissive biogenic carbon dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati 781035, India. Fax: +91 361 2279909

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Nanochemistry Laboratory, Physical Sciences Division, Institute of Advanced Study in Science and Technology, Paschim Boragaon, Garchuk, Guwahati-781035, India. Fax: +91 361 2279909

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
There has been considerable interest in dual emissive carbon quantum dots because of their exciting photoluminescence properties for varied applications. This work demonstrates a dual emissive system derived from a biogenic source, betalain extracted from beetroot. Different betalain carbon dots were prepared utilizing varying temperatures and time. The photoluminescence properties of
BT − CD
5
160
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 160 ℃ for 5 min) and
BT − CD
10
180
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 180 ℃ for 10 mins) were investigated in detail using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and time-resolved PL decay studies. Interestingly the emission spectra are different and possess multi-emissive nature owing to the involvement of different surface states on the CDs. Further the PL response of
BT − CD
5
160
and,
BT − CD
10
180
in the presence of triethylamine (TEA), an aliphatic amine and, diphenylamine (DPA), an aromatic amine was found to be different when studied for both λext < 400 nm and > 400 nm. While the emission obtained from
BT − CD
5
160
emerges from the aromatic carbon core and also from the functional groups attached to the carbon dots’ surface, the emission in
BT − CD
10
180
is fully governed by the surface traps and defects obtained from the functionalities present. Hence, such investigated photoluminescence studies of the origin of emission will help exploit the dual emissive behaviour of biogenic carbon dots for extensive application in ratiometric sensing and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been achieving acclamation for their unique properties such as chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, good dispersibility, lack of optical blinking, and low photobleaching, which gradually helped in carving a niche in the family of nanomaterials. The presence of these features in CDs has gained dominance over semiconductor-based quantum dots and fluorescent organic dyes in the area of optoelectronics, [1] photocatalysis, [2] drug-delivery, [3] bioimaging, [4] sensing, [5] solar light harvesting, [6] out of several. Photoluminescence (PL) is a phenomenon imbibed in carbon dots and has been a fascinating feature for material chemists because of its perplexing mechanism of origin. Although several speculations were carried out and documented in literature, there is no such universal interpretation of the underlying origin of PL mechanism of carbon dots. The reason beneath is the variability and diversity occurring during the carbon dot formation which directly depends on the raw materials chosen and the synthesis protocol used. On the basis of structural entity, carbon dots are described as sp2 conjugated carbogenic system surrounded by different oxygen-related functional group moieties, termed as the surface state. Considering this structure, a group came out of the fact that the sp2 carbon domain inside the sp3 carbon containing functional groups facilitate in radiative electron-hole recombination process leading to the origin of PL [7]. Pan et al. proposed that the origin of PL might be due to the free zigzag sites with a carbine-like triplet ground state [8]. On the other hand, Krysmann et al. suggested the PL mechanism originates from the surface states localized in edge of sp2 carbon [9]. Wen et al. demonstrated via femtosecond resolved up-conversion technique that PL arises from two spectral overlapped bands which can be attributed to intrinsic and extrinsic fluorescence that are linked to sp2 carbon domain and the surface states respectively [10]. From these findings, the anticipated reasons of the PL origin might arise either from the inner carbon core domain or, from the various surface functional group domain attached to the CDs or, the synergistic contribution of both the domain resulting in edge-related defects. The presence of a heteroatom like N, S, B or P also influences the emission mechanism of CDs. In case of N-containing CDs, the type of N-species formed decides the role involved in PL emission. Only aromatic N that gets incorporated into the carbon framework are taken as dopants and is said to introduce defects into the system, [11] whereas aliphatic N acts as surface passivation that further adds to the radiative recombination process by inducing new kinds of surface and edge states into the CDs system [11,12].

Generally, the PL of the CDs is based on a single emission system; [13,14] however, a few studies report the emergence of a dual emission system. Dual emission is a phenomenon where two emission maxima occur at a given excitation wavelength. The presence of spectral heterogeneity in terms of multiple or simultaneous excitations occurring in the CDs system due to multiple emission sites, [15,16] variable functional groups, [17] and the presence of heteroatoms [18] are assumed to be accountable for dual emission peaks. Most of the reports have demonstrated dual emissions either by mixing two reactants or by doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) or sulphur (S). For example, Wang et al. obtained carbon dot-based dual emission fluorescent nanohybrids by coupling of glutathione-stabilised gold nanoclusters and amino-functionalised CDs [19]. The system was used as nanothermometry device for measurement of temperature and its gradient inside a living cell. Another dual emissive system was obtained when citric acid CDs and Rhodamine B were grafted onto silica nanoparticles [20]. While Sun et al. prepared dual emissive CDs by co-doping N and P [21], followed by Guan et al. synthesizing the same by co-doping N and S [22]. Recently, our group has reported the formation of dual emission fluorescence peak from a single carbon dot system synthesized from a natural source, carotenoids [23] which was a breakthrough finding in a carbon dot system. The study demonstrated the conversion of a carotenoid system having a single emission peak to carbon dot system having a double emission peak via tuning the conjugated electronic state of the carotenoid.

This work also demonstrates a dual emissive system derived from a single biogenic source, betalain extracted from beetroot. Biogenic sources provide a diversified chemical environment that might result in multipeak fluorescence emission from CDs generated from a single system only. The different chemical environments formed in the CDs are recognized from the structural characterizations carried out. Stating the pathways of fluorescence emission is increasingly common. However, identifying the functionalities involved in the origin of these multi-emissive peaks and assigning their emission pathways in a diversified carbon dot environment seems quite challenging. Understanding the origin of multi-emissive peaks is necessary for multifunctional imaging biosystem [24] and ratiometric sensing [25]. So far, there are no such reports of comprehending PL mechanisms for biogenic CDs system. Therefore, in this study, we attempt to elucidate the functional origin of these multi-emissive peaks and understand the different radiative pathways involved throughout the emission process, which was done via analyzing the optical data recorded from the absorption, excitation, emission and time-resolved lifetime spectra. This work also describes the influence of temperature and time in moulding the chemical environment of the dual emissive carbon dot system. It needs to be mentioned that due to the marked differences in their optical properties, the characterizations and analysis of only
BT − CD
5
160
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 160 ℃ for 5 min) and
BT − CD
10
180
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 180 ℃ for 10 mins) are discussed throughout the paper.

Beetroot (Beta vulgaris) was brought and collected from the local market of Guwahati, India. Triethylamine, Diphenylamine and Methanol were purchased from Merck, India. Absolute ethanol was purchased from Nucleus Scientific, India. Milli Q water was used throughout the experiments.

In this work, carbon dot was synthesized from betalains, a natural compound derived from red beetroot via maceration method. In the process, the coarsely ground beetroot was subjected to solvent extraction (80% methanol) to obtain the crude betalain extract. The carbon dot preparation proceeded, taking the betalain extract as the carbon source. In the procedure, 100 mg of the betalain extract was mixed with 10 mL of 70% ethanol, chosen as the dispersion medium. The mixture was transferred to a G30 reaction vial to undergo microwave irradiation at different temperatures and time, 160, 170 and, 180 ℃ for 5 and 10 min. After that, the purification of the samples were carried out by centrifuging at 7500 rpm for 15 min to get rid of the residues, followed by filtering with a 0.4 µm syringe filter to remove other larger particles. Finally, the obtained carbon dots were finally stored at 4 ℃ for characterizations and further experimental uses. The carbon dots are designated as
BT − CD
Y
X
, where X and Y signify the parameters, temperature, and time respectively, set during the synthesis. The power and the pressure of the microwave reactor were between 21 and 25 W and 10–16 bar throughout the synthesis.

The pH of the prepared carbon dots solution was adjusted to lower and higher values by using hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solution, respectively, and checked by pH meter. The carbon dots under low pH and high pH are designated as
aBT − CD
Y
X
and
bBT − CD
Y
X
respectively.

In other experiments, titration with different amines was performed taking particular concentrations of triethylamine and Diphenylamine and adding aliquots of these solutions to the carbon dots solutions and recording the photoluminescence spectra.

Monowave reactor (Monowave 200) of Anton Paar was used to synthesize the carbon dots. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurement was taken to determine the particle size of the prepared carbon dots using the Malvern ZS90 instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution TEM, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were recorded for studying the morphology and microstructure of the carbon dots (JEOL TEM-2100 plus model, 200 kV operating voltage). X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) spectra was recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.54056 Å). Absorption spectra were taken in Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer UV-2600 and, the emission and excitation spectra were carried out on Jasco spectrofluorometer FP-8300. The FTIR spectra of the carbon dots were recorded on a Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrophotometer using KBr pellet. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on ESCALAB Xi+ instrument of Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd., UK with a monochromatic X-ray source Al Kα excitation. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on Picosecond Time-Resolved Fluorimeter (LifeSpec II, Edinburg Instruments) with 375 nm, 405 nm and 445 nm pulse laser excitation sources. The decay profiles were fitted by the multi-exponential function provided by the F980 software to elucidate the lifetime dynamics of the carbon dots.

Betalains extracted from beetroot (Beta vulgaris) are nitrogen-containing water-soluble plant pigments that mainly constitute two structural units: red-violet betacyanin and yellow-orange betaxanthin [26]. The presence of their characteristic absorption maxima at 538 nm and 480 nm for betacyanin and betaxanthin, respectively, in the UV–visible spectrum of the betalain extract (Figure S1) indicates the successful extraction of betalains from beetroot [26]. Betalains are known to be susceptible to high temperatures. Therefore, when an aqueous ethanolic solution of betalain extract was subjected to microwave treatment, destruction of the betalain pigments occurred, which was understood by the alteration of the characteristic reddish-violet colour of the betalain solution to yellowish golden/brown. The resulting solution gave bright blue to greenish fluorescence under the illumination of UV light which directly implied the formation of carbon dots (Scheme S1). It could be assumed that decarboxylation, elimination of glycosides and subsequently aromatization took place that ultimately eventuated to the carbon core formation [27]. As mentioned in the experimental section, by varying the two parameters, temperature and time, a total of four betalain carbon dots were prepared, namely,
BT − CD
5
160
,
BT − CD
5
170
,
BT − CD
5
180
and,
BT − CD
10
180
. For further confirmation of the carbon dot formation, DLS measurements and TEM images were taken for the low and high temperature BT-CDs. Figure S2 and S3 display the DLS profile, TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
respectively, showing the dots to be well dispersed and distributed within the range of 1–10 nm. The HR-TEM of
BT − CD
10
180
reveals the presence of lattice fringes whose d-spacing obtained is 2.07 Å correlating to that of (100) plane of graphite [14,28] ( Fig. 1a). The image also unfolds the presence of both crystalline and amorphous phases of the dots. This is evident from the SAED image (Fig. 1b) and a broad diffraction peak at 20⁰ in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) which corresponds to the interlayer spacing of 4.33 Å, indicating the poor crystallinity of
BT − CD
10
180
. This also demonstrate mixed phase of
BT − CD
10
180
.
However, a total amorphous nature is observed for
BT − CD
5
160
, as proven by the SAED image given in Figure S4.

Structural characterizations such as FT-IR and XPS are performed for these two CDs in order to learn about their formation and functionalities involved. The FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 2a) of both these BT-CDs showed some common stretching frequencies at 1668 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, and 1054 cm−1, which corresponds to CO, C-N, and C-O-C functionalities, respectively. The intense sharp peak at 1384 cm−1 is a characteristic feature found only in the IR spectrum of N-doped CDs [29]. Another peak at 1594 cm−1 in
BT − CD
5
160
can be assigned to N-H bending vibration, which is otherwise seen diminished in case of
BT − CD
10
180
. The presence of free N-H groups induces H-bonding in the system, and its decrease is reflected by the shifting of N-H stretch to a higher frequency from 3368 cm−1 (of
BT − CD
5
160
) to 3389 cm−1 (of
BT − CD
10
180
). Nevertheless, the broadness of these peaks can be attributed to O-H stretch, intermolecularly H-bonded and overlapping with that of N-H stretch for both the BT-CDs. The peaks at 1132 cm−1 and 1105 cm−1 of
BT − CD
5
160
both correspond to C-O stretching vibration. All the absorptions occurring below 1000 cm−1 are due to CC and =C-H bending vibrations of the BT-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of the betalain extract (BT) itself was also taken to understand and compare the changes occurred after the carbon dot formation. The spectrum is given in Figure S5 of SI along with its comparative explanation.

For intricate understanding of chemical bonds associated with C, N and O elements present in the BT-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out for the two BT-CDs. Fig. 2b shows a stacked XPS survey scan of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
, displaying the respective binding energy values and whose atomic weight% are tabulated in the Table S1. The high-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s, and O1s are displayed in Fig. 2(c, d, e) for
BT − CD
5
160
and in Fig. 2(f, g, h) for
BT − CD
10
180
assigning the probable functionalities to the obtained binding energy values as per the literature. For
BT − CD
5
160
the C1s band can be deconvoluted into four peaks such as sp2 hybridized carbons (CC), sp3 hybridized carbons (C-O/C-N), epoxy carbon (C-O-C), and carbonyl carbons (CO)/CN having binding energy values 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 287 eV, respectively [30]. The N1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks representing pyrrolic N/amino N and graphitic N at 399.6 eV and 401.6 eV, respectively whereas, O1s into three peaks containing CO, C-OH, and C-O-C (epoxy oxygen) groups at 531.7 eV, 532.5 eV, and 533.3 eV, respectively [31].
BT − CD
10
180
has similar deconvolution spectra shown in Fig. 2(f, g, h), whose binding energy values are given in Table S2. The presence of relatively higher intensity of C-N and CN along with CC (Fig. 2c) is indicative of consequential doping of N both at the core as well as at the surface of
BT − CD
5
160
. A significant increase of CC intensity along with the decrease in intensity of C-O/C-N and CO/CN functionalities (Fig. 2f) implies carbonization of
BT − CD
10
180
with the increase of temperature. The decrease of CO is also reflected in the O1s deconvoluted spectrum of
BT − CD
10
180
(Fig. 2e) when compared to that of
BT − CD
5
160
(Fig. 2h). However, the increase of C-O-C peak intensity in case of
BT − CD
10
180
(Fig. 2f and, 2 h) suggests more cyclization occurring at higher temperatures during the carbon dot formation. On the other hand, the decrease of C-OH peak intensity of O1s deconvoluted spectra (Fig. 2h) is in agreement with the weak absorptions of C-O stretches in the FT-IR spectra of
BT − CD
10
180
(Fig. 2b). In other words, the number of C-OH decreases at higher temperatures, resulting in shifting the O-H stretch to a higher frequency. The N1s components can differentiate the type of doping involved during the carbon dot formation. Graphitic N generally resides within the core of the CDs, whereas the pyrrolic N group is usually present both at the core and surface of CDs [32]. Presence of N-component in the carbon core is said to induce disorder/defect in the lattice [33]. At higher temperature and time, accumulation of carbon functionalities rapidly occurred limiting N-doping which is understood from the marked decrease in Graphitic N content by contributing to the surface terminations as pyrrolic N group (Fig. 2g), thereby reducing the disorderly within the carbon core by creating some crystallinity in
BT − CD
10
180
. This deduction is compatible with the HR-TEM of
BT − CD
10
180
(Fig. 1). It is to be noted that pyrrolic N does not give any prominent absorptions in the IR spectrum [29]. Therefore, the assigning of N-H bend in
BT − CD
5
160
(Fig. 2a) is assumed to arise from amides; as a result, the binding energy value of 399.6 eV is given for amidic N along with pyrrolic N (Fig. 2d). At higher temperature, owing to cyclization and instability of - NH2 groups, the binding energy value of 399.8 eV is allotted to only pyrrolic N (Fig. 2g) as evident from the very weak absorption of N-H bend in the FT-IR spectrum of
BT − CD
10
180
. For clarity, the %area of all the peaks of the deconvoluted spectra of the BT-CDs is compared and represented in Figure S6. Therefore, it can be generalized that both the BT-CDs are composed of π-conjugated aromatic domains with different functional moieties such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, -CONH2, pyrrolic N at varying intensities lying at the edges and surface of the carbon dots. These moieties either act as defects or trap states which is foreseen to modulate the optical properties of the BT-CDs.

To explore the effect of the created surface groups and defects on the BT-CDs, optical properties were studied. Fig. 3A displays the absorption spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
and,
BT − CD
10
180
. A common band at the far UV region represents π→π * transition of sp2 conjugated carbon network originating from the core state of both BT-CDs. A hypsochromic shift is seen for
BT − CD
10
180
at this region that is assumed to occur due to the increased epoxy-O content [34]. The absorbance at the near UV region at 299 nm of
BT − CD
5
160
represents n→π * transition of O and N-containing functionalities located at the edge of the carbon dot structure, [35] whereas a dual band at 270 and 289 nm is obtained in case of
BT − CD
10
180
which is assumed to occur from a mixed contribution of π→π * and n→π * transitions [35–37]. Such kind of absorption spectra is speculated for N-doped CDs [36]. Besides, both the CDs have their low energy absorption extending onto the visible region (more prominently, a weak band at 410 nm, in case of
BT − CD
5
160
) can be ascribed to arise from lower energy states of N-related functional groups on the surface of CDs [35].

The emission spectra of
BT − CD
5
160
and,
BT − CD
10
180
represented in Fig. 3 (B) and (C) respectively, is found to be quite intriguing. Both of them differed in their photoluminescent behaviour in a way that
BT − CD
5
160
exhibited irregularity in their emission nature compared to that of
BT − CD
10
180
, when studied through a series of wavelengths ranging from 350 nm to 460 nm. In
BT − CD
5
160
, three emission peaks are present at different excitation wavelengths. The peak at 460 nm on excitation at 360 nm, might arise due to absorptions below 300 nm.

A dual emission fluorescence peak centered around ~490 nm and 530 nm emerge at excitations greater than 430 nm. It is worth noticing that the double fluorescence peak display different behavior throughout the emission spectra. The first peak at ~490 nm depicts the usual excitation-dependent PL behaviour, whereas the second peak shows excitation-independent PL behaviour. On the other hand, the emission spectra of
BT − CD
10
180
(Fig. 3(C)) display a comparatively smoothened spectra with two emission peaks as dual fluorescence spectra at all excitation wavelengths studied. Eventually, the first peak of the dual emissive peaks diminishes for both the BT-CDs as less electrons are drained into the conduction bands of the BT-CDs at moving towards more longer wavelengths. Such variance and invariance in the PL emission spectra of these CDs indicate the multi-emissive nature owing to the involvement of different surface states on the CDs. The formation of dual emissive peaks in both the BT-CDs is captivating in a way that it is a consequence of the presence of heteroatom, N that molds the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs of the surrounding functional groups on the CDs’ surface. The process may occur such that soon after the excited electrons gives out electronic transitions, the electrons relax via non-radiative mechanism, and then again recombines with other edge/surface functional groups via e-h radiative process induced by the different types of surface functionalities present. The absorption and emission spectra of
BT − CD
5
170
and ,
BT − CD
5
180
are given in Figure S7.

The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of both the
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
were recorded to unveil the excited states involved. Fig. 4 is the PLE spectra of the BT-CDs and is found to significantly vary from each other when studied with different emission wavelengths ranging from 460 to 570 nm. In
BT − CD
5
160
, two distinct excitation transitions are found at 360 nm and 433–440 nm in which the peak at 360 nm declines gradually and the other peak abruptly rise and then falls off with the increment of λems. This clearly hints out the presence of two excited states. The PLE peak at 360 nm that corresponds to the emission peak at 460 nm may arise from the intrinsic carbon core domain. The other λems dependent peak, ranging from 433 to 440 nm, is assumed to emerge from the dual emissive peaks of the emission spectrum that are expected to occur owing to O- and N-surface traps/defects, as evident from the absorption spectrum. Therefore, it is likely that there is an energy transfer from the carbon core to the surface states that led to the declining of the first PLE peak. On the other hand,
BT − CD
10
180
has one PLE transition where its peak position is dependent on λems. The peak position initially increases and then decreases with increment in λems. This peak also originates from the double emission spectra of
BT − CD
10
180
. Based on the PLE bands found throughout the visible region, the PLE spectra of both the BT-CDs is indicative of multiple emissive surface states that is responsible for the λex-dependent PL behaviour [9,38]. More precisely, this dependency might be associated to the presence of different fluorophores formed by the synergistic hybridization of O-/N-containing surface functional groups with the π-conjugated system of the carbon core that help in tuning of the PL property. The excitation spectra of
BT − CD
5
170
and ,
BT − CD
5
180
are given in Figure S8.

To further interpret the multiple emission sites involved, PL lifetimes of the BT-CDs were recorded at two different excitations, one < 400 nm and the other > 400 nm. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method was used to find out the decay times of individual components, and calculating the weight of the constituent components along with the distribution of the emissive states. All the decay profiles were fitted using multi-exponential decay function:
F(t) = Σ Bi exp (-t/τi)
where τi and Bi denote PL lifetimes and amplitudes of each component.

The time-resolved decay profiles of the BT-CDs were recorded based on single and dual emission peaks found in both of the PL spectra. For
BT − CD
5
160
, two laser excitation sources of 375 nm and 445 nm were used whereas, for
BT − CD
10
180
, 375 nm and 405 nm laser sources were used. Fit results of both the BT-CDs are summarized in Table 1. All the decay profiles were fitted by tri-exponential functions on a timescale of hundreds of picoseconds (ps) to nanosecond (ns), confirming the presence of three emission states on the BT-CDs. The increase of τav while moving towards a longer wavelength suggests spectral heterogeneity in both of the BT-CDs, which corresponds to the invariable emission spectra of the BT-CDs. Further, it can be speculated from the table that the lifetimes of each of the constituents generated can be divided into three parts: two faster components, τ1 ranging in hundreds of ps, and τ2 of 1–2 ns and the other intermediate component, τ3 of 4–7 ns. Lifetime < 100 ps is assumed to occur from the intrinsic carbonic cores or intraband transitions [39]. Therefore, the occurrence of comparatively faster lifetime component (τ1 = 74 ps) and shorter τav of 0.49 ns at λext 360 nm for
BT − CD
5
160
correlates with the prominence of emission peak due to the intrinsic carbon core domains. The lifetime component ranging from a few hundreds of ps to ~2.5 ns has been proposed to originate from edge states which is an interplay between the sp2-carbon core and the tethered surface functional groups along with the surface exposed nitrogen in the form of pyrrolic N group [40]. Whereas, the fraction of lifetimes greater than 5 ns is designated to originate from the functionalities attached to the surface states, as reflected by the extension of the absorption tail above 400 nm in the absorption spectra of the BT-CDs. These include oxygen related functional groups having non-bonding electrons. The higher contribution of τ3 can be correlated to the higher content of O-element found in XPS survey scan. Henceforth, two important points can be deduced from Table 1: (i) considering the greater weightage of τ2 and τ3 counterparts, the BT-CDs have their emissions prominently dominated by the edge-related defects and surface traps by the virtue of N- and O-related functional groups, except that of
BT − CD
5
160
, which exhibited emissions due to sp2 carbon core at shorter wavelength, (ii) pyrrolic N is said to generate surface defects, contributing to the radiative recombination of localized e-h pairs that results in generating shorter lifetimes. This is evidenced by the reduction of τav of
BT − CD
10
180
owing to its higher content of pyrrolic N [41], as analysed from the XPS data. The photoluminescence decay profiles of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
of the monitored excitation wavelengths are provided in Figure S9 of Supplementary Information.

The pH dependence fluorescence study was carried out on both
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
at < 400 nm and > 400 nm excitation wavelengths to understand the influence of edge/surface functional groups present on the BT-CDs. Figs. 5a and 5b shows the effect of acidic and basic pH on
BT − CD
5
160
at two different excitation wavelengths.

At lower excitation (360 nm), the PL quenches at acidic pH but enhances at basic pH. As mentioned, the emission of
BT − CD
5
160
at lower wavelength is dominated by the carbogenic cores.

The presence of substantial amount of graphitic N, revealed by the XPS analysis, also contributes to the PL emission via electron doping effect by the lone pair of electrons on the graphitic N. In acidic medium, the lone pair of electrons might undergo H+ attack, blocking some emissions resulting in quenching of PL intensity. C-N and CN bonds are said to improve PL emission inside carbonous core. Under basic condition, a situation might occur such that OH- attacks the proton of pyrrolic N, resulting in the creation of negative charge on the N-atom subsequently enhancing the radiative recombination of localized e-h pairs. At higher excitation wavelength (450 nm), the quenching behaviour is shown for both low and high pH, with manifold quenching of PL intensity at higher pH compared to that of lower pH. The functional groups attached to the carbon dot’s edges/surface dominate the emissions at longer wavelength. The tethered functional groups vary from electron-donating to electron-withdrawing nature, where the former facilitates radiative recombination of e-h pairs, and the latter inhibits. At acidic medium, owing to the protonation of –OH and –CONH2 group, electron donation from the surface domain to the carbon core is hindered, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which otherwise donates its lone pair of electrons to the conduction band of the carbon core at pH 7. A prominent quenching induced by
BT − CD
5
160
under basic medium is expected to occur due to the formation of non-radiative complexes, thereby blocking the electrons from jumping over to the conduction band.

Figs. 5c and 5d portrays the changes in PL behaviour of
BT − CD
10
180
under acidic and basic pH at lower and higher excitation wavelengths, respectively. The changes are found to be similar in a way that at pH 1, the PL intensity increases, whereas at pH 11, a drastic quenching of PL intensity is obtained for both the excitations studied. The PL behaviour of
BT − CD
10
180
is more dominated by the surface functional groups, out of which epoxy and pyrrolic N groups stand out to contribute more to the carbon dot system compared to other groups. The epoxy-O located at the edge or basal planes might undergo ring-opening reaction under H+ attack at low pH, altering its nature from electron-withdrawing to electron-donating. In the event, the electrons could jump to the conduction band, enhancing the radiative recombination process. However, the increase of PL intensity is comparatively less at higher excitation due to an opposing factor contributed by other surface functional groups such as C-OH that particularly dominates at longer wavelength region, as explained in the lifetime table section. The substantial decrease of PL intensity at both excitations under basic conditions can be reasoned to form non-radiative complexes. Also, a situation might occur such that under excessive hydroxyl ions, ample donation of electrons would take place from the surface HOMO to the valence band of carbon core, resulting in subsequent filling of holes. This, in turn, is effective in cutting back the radiative recombination of e-h pairs from the valence band to conduction band.

Further, the response of the emissive peaks for λex > 400 nm and λex < 400 nm of biogenic carbon dots prepared at two different temperature conditions were investigated in presence of two types of amines and titrated at various concentrations with the BT-CDs. The amines selected are of two different categories: triethylamine (TEA), an aliphatic amine and, diphenylamine (DPA), an aromatic amine. Figs. 6A and 6B records the PL emission of
BT − CD
5
160
at different molar concentrations of TEA ranging from 20 mM to 50.6 mM at λex 360 nm and 450 nm respectively. The response was such that at lower excitation (360 nm), fluorescence intensity enhances with increasing concentration of TEA whereas, at higher excitation (450 nm), the trend is opposite, that is, fluorescence intensity decreases gradually at the same concentration range of TEA. In fact, the response was similar to that of
BT − CD
5
160
at basic medium (pH 11). This vividly illustrates the different emissive sites dominating independently at different excitation wavelengths.

At λex 360 nm, dominated by the carbonous core, the presence of lone pair of nitrogen on TEA contributes to the conduction band of the carbon core, enhancing the radiative recombination process thereby leading to the turning-on of the PL intensity. Also, the bulky ethyl groups of TEA might create a passivating effect on
BT − CD
5
160
contributing in the increase of PL intensity as its concentration gradually increases. At λex 450 nm dominated by the surface functionalities, a situation might occur such that some non-radiative complexes might form between TEA and edge/surface functional groups, thus reducing the PL intensity, which otherwise would have been a favourable factor for enhancing the PL intensity. Figs. 6C and 6D shows the PL emission response of
BT − CD
10
180
at different molar concentrations of TEA ranging from 20 mM to 50.6 mM at λex 380 nm and 420 nm respectively. Both the spectra showed a gradual ‘turn-on’ in fluorescence intensity while increasing the concentration of TEA. This clearly states the near-to-uniformity in the surface states of
BT − CD
10
180
where the PL emission spectrum is fully governed by edge-related defects and surface functional groups. Therefore, the gradually ehnanced PL intensity at increasing the TEA concentration can be attributed to surface passivating effect.

Fig. 7 shows the PL emission response of the BT-CDs system at λex < 400 nm and > 400 nm after introducing DPA at the same molar concentration range from 20 mM to 50.6 mM. No such prominent change of PL intensity occurred in
BT − CD
5
160
at λex 360 nm with increasing concentration of DPA (Fig. 7A). The phenyl groups of DPA is expected to create negative inductive effect making the lone pair of nitrogen not easily available for donation to the conduction band of carbon dot system. Whereas, at λex 450 nm, quenching of PL intensity of
BT − CD
5
160
is seen with increasing concentration of DPA. On the other hand, both Figs. 7C and 7D show gradual increment in fluorescence intensity in case of
BT − CD
10
180
at both excitations when subjected to different molar concentration of DPA, further proving the uniformity in the emission sites of
BT − CD
10
180
.

The next batch of titrations with TEA and DPA was carried out with acidic and basic BT-CDs to further validate the nature of the BT-CDs. The explanations of each of the PL responses are provided in the Supporting Information along with the representative Figures S10 and S11. From all the spectra obtained, it can be justified that
BT − CD
5
160
has different types of emission sites independently dominating at different excitation wavelengths; the proofs of which are provided by the addition of two different amines, responding in opposite manner at two given excitation wavelengths. While
BT − CD
10
180
has emissive sites mostly governed by the surface functional groups where the proofs are given by its similar PL responses after being subjected to two different amines.

Henceforth, by summarizing all the results, the mechanistic picture of the multi-emissive nature of
BT − CD
5
160
and
BT − CD
10
180
can be drawn out as follows: (1) the emission window of
BT − CD
5
160
comprises of two segments; one of which emerges from the aromatic carbon core and the other, from the functional groups attached to the carbon dots’ surface. The evidence of the emission arising from the carbon core domain was given by the peak at 360 nm of the excitation spectra along with its very short lifetime (74 ps) of one of the components (τ1), (2) the emission window of
BT − CD
10
180
is fully governed by the surface traps and defects obtained from the functionalities present, which was understood by the disappearance of the peak in the excitation spectra at shorter wavelength region, (3) the double fluorescence peak at λext > 400 nm is seen to differ from each other. The excitation dependent behaviour of the first peak is expected due to the modulation of the emissions from the intrinsic bands to the edge states, which can be deduced from the increase in lifetime value of τ1 and τ2 components at excitations > 400 nm. The second peak, exhibiting the excitation independent behaviour depicts the emissions arising from the tethered surface functionalities, irrespective of the experimental parameters, (4) the pathway for the appearance of dual emissive peaks can be proposed as follows- (a) the electrons after being photoexcited can undergo synergistic hybridisation via π→π * transitions of sp2 conjugated aromatic domains to n→π * transitions of the groups located at the edges such as, pyrrolic N and epoxy O, (b) the excited electrons are then relaxed to CO energy levels via non-radiative processes, (c) the excited electrons gets again reactivated via two process. One might be through direct recombination process owing to the surface traps induced by the various O-related functional groups. The other pathway might occur via intersystem crossing (C π * → N π * and, N π * → O π * ), followed by vibrational relaxation and eventually radiative recombination occurs [42]. Such pathways are actually responsible for λems-dependent excitation spectra. The presence of N in the BT-CDs is seemed to act as a mediator in creating such double fluorescence peaks. Scheme 8 summaries various radiative pathways responsible for the PL emissions of the BT-CDs.

To summarize, we have synthesized N-doped carbon dots (BT-CDs) using a single biogenic precursor derived from beetroot extract (known as betalains) via microwave assisted method by varying temperature and time. The carbon dot formation was confirmed by Uv-visible spectrophotometer, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, X-ray diffraction and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. It was observed that the photoluminescence properties of
BT − CD
5
160
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 160 °C for 5 mins) and
BT CD
10
180
(betalain carbon dots prepared at 180 °C for 10 mins) including different pH conditions possesses multi-emissive nature owing to the involvement of different surface states on the CDs. Further the PL response of
BT − CD
5
160
and,
BT − CD
10
180
in presence of triethylamine (TEA), an aliphatic amine and, diphenylamine (DPA), an aromatic amine at λex 360 nm and 450 nm respectively, was found to be opposite for both the excitations studied. While the emission obtained from
BT − CD
5
160
emerges from the aromatic carbon core and the other, from the functional groups attached to the carbon dots’ surface, the emission in
BT − CD
10
180
is governed by the surface traps and defects obtained from the functionalities present. Hence this study helps in understanding the origin of emission from dual emisive biogenic carbon quantum dots which are cheap, environment friendly and non-toxic for varied applications like multifunctional imaging biosystem, ratiometric sensing optoelectronic applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thank IASST for in-house project. The authors also thank SAIC, IASST; SAIF, CSIR-NEIST; CIF, IIT-Guwahati for instrumentation facilities. AD thanks IASST for fellowship.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, R., Li, L., & Jin, W.J. (2017). Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence. Carbon, 111, 133–141.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy-level structure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang, L., Ji, R., Li, X., Teng, K.S., & Lau, S.P. (2013). Energy-level structure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 1(32), 4908–4915.
